what_it_does: This file is a noticeboard block item that records the author's assessment of which product capabilities are ready to ship soon versus which remain substantial builds, warning against treating unfinished semantic, access, and anchoring features as near-term deliverables.
observations: YAML frontmatter includes id, address, type block, source claude_code, channel dragnet-development, and a part_of link to board://item-eb5d71e7.
Created and updated timestamps are identical, with a single history entry marking it as filed.
Body is titled 'P11 · What I'd actually trust to ship vs. not' and splits content into two bold sections.

The 'Could be real soon' section names an existing loop (tap-a-block → composer → commented_on edge → lead reply) tied to doc_review_server.py and cc_board, plus draft queue, submit-all, inbox overlay, and MSG2 UI fixes.

The 'Do NOT pretend is near' section lists six capabilities: semantic document tree, slide-to-scale gesture, live element→source resolution, member-gated data access + channel convergence, self-resolving editable locations, and durable anchoring across edits.
It explicitly warns that items #1, #4, and #6 are the ones that could 'strand the repo' if sequenced naively.
standouts: Explicit 'trust to ship vs. not' risk framing rather than a neutral feature list.
Identifies an already-running technical loop as the safe foundation to build outward from.
Labels six capabilities as full builds despite being presented elsewhere as 'finishing what exists'.
Direct warning that three of those capabilities could strand the repository.
conventions: Follows the noticeboard item YAML frontmatter schema with id, address, type, source, channel, links, and history.
Uses markdown headings and bold text for emphasis.
References another board item via a board:// URI in links.
Uses P11 prefix in the title, suggesting a numbered series or milestone post.
Uses #1–#6 shorthand to refer to listed items.
concerns: Evaluative and prescriptive language ('Do NOT pretend', 'strand the repo') is strong and not substantiated within the file itself.
References external artifacts (doc_review_server.py, cc_board, MSG2) without defining them for a reader who lacks context.
The #1–#6 shorthand is ambiguous: it is unclear whether these map to issue numbers, prior posts, or internal identifiers.
Created and updated timestamps are identical, so the file shows no subsequent refinement or rebuttal.
notes: A risk-opinion note arguing the team should extend an already-working comment/composer loop rather than commit to unfinished semantic, gating, and anchoring work.
questions: What is MSG2 and how does it relate to the listed UI fixes?
What does 'strand the repo' mean in concrete terms for this codebase?
What is the relationship between this item and board://item-eb5d71e7?
What criteria distinguish 'mostly exists' from 'is a build' in this assessment?
intention_signals: It appears to be trying to redirect planning toward a proven, shippable feedback loop and to prevent the team from committing prematurely to large cross-cutting features that could destabilize the repository.
novelty: The blunt 'trust to ship / do not pretend is near' framing, combined with an explicit repo-stranding warning and the contrast of small UI polish against dangerous structural work.
contribution: Captures a scoped, opinionated milestone recommendation and a risk checklist that a fused system could preserve as a planning signal without ranking it above other sources.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard item titled 'P11 · What I'd actually trust to ship vs. not' that separates near-shippable comment/composer/UI improvements from longer-term semantic, gesture, access, and anchoring capabilities and warns that naive sequencing of the latter could strand the repository.
intention_for_embedding: To steer the team toward extending an existing working feedback loop and away from treating unfinished structural features as imminent deliverables.
